data.title: Moncler: Critical Phase Is Over, But It's No Longer A Buy
data.description:
In late 2024, I published a series of buy articles on Moncler (OTCPK:MONRF), an Italian haute couture company that was collapsing due to the tightening economic environment. The entire haute couture industry was in trouble (and still is), but after only a few months Moncler seems to have already made up for lost ground.

The annual report released yesterday was a success on all fronts, and the market rewarded Moncler with a gain of 7%. Anyway, the stock was already on the rise, and today at $70.62 per share I no longer think it is a buy. In my opinion this company is among the best in the world in its industry, which is why I have included it in my portfolio, but it may take some time before I increase my position. I have enough invested around $55 per share, and I don't think it is worth increasing my average buy price.

Highlights FY2024/Q4 2024

As you know, the main problem in this industry has been China's economic meltdown. Luxury goods have a very high demand in the east, and with the Chinese real estate crisis, the situation has changed. However, in Q4 2024, Moncler showed encouraging signs of growth, and analysts are beginning to be more optimistic about an economic recovery for the Chinese. If China fully recovers from the real estate crisis, so will the luxury industry. This explains why the market is so bullish on Moncler while a few months ago no one was considering it.

Let us now look in detail at the performance of the company's two main brands, Moncler and Stone Island.

Moncler had an 8% increase in revenues on both an annual and quarterly basis. What is important to note here is the increasing share that Asia is getting on the whole portfolio. Moncler has actually never really been in trouble in this respect, unlike competitors like LVMH and Kering, which is why I considered it a buy. As of today, however, with the China situation improving, revenues are growing double digits again in Asia: +11% vs. Q4 2023 and FY2023.

According to management, this positive result was due to a series of choices and investments made in China in recent years; so it is not just a matter of the country's economic recovery. Moncler knows Chinese consumers well and is helped by a local team to understand their needs. It also holds incredible events throughout the year to increase its visibility, such as Moncler Genius in Shanghai.

If Moncler has been resilient to China's adverse economic environment, it is because its competitive advantage in this country is enormous. When the Chinese think of a high-quality brand for winter, Moncler is the first one that comes to their mind: there are no real competitors in outerwear.

And I think we have been able to develop a trust and quality perception of Moncler that is very, very high in the market. But this is not the only element. I think we have also developed. We are one of the few brands where we – or maybe even the only go to brand in China for luxury outerwear. So really, when the call is coming and when people thinking about full winter, we are absolutely top of mind. In the – and there is not a real competitor in the market, at least not in our positioning.

Roberto Eggs – Chief Business Strategy and Global Market Officer

It is quite ironic how analysts have been concerned about Asia, but it is Americas and EMEA that are the slowest growing business segments. In particular, EMEA grew only 3% on a quarterly basis, while on an annual basis it improved by 5%: meaning that growth in EMEA is struggling. Italy was the country in EMEA with the best growth, but it seems that the main problem concerns a lower turnout of Chinese tourists in EMEA.

We still are missing some of the tourists we had in the past, especially in Europe because, yes, there is a presence of Chinese in Europe, but absolutely not to the extent that we had before, there is a 10% gap in the weight of Chinese in Europe, in 2024 compared to 2019.

Roberto Eggs – Chief Business Strategy and Global Market Officer

When the economic situation in China improves, perhaps there will be more Chinese tourists and Moncler can benefit.

In terms of distribution channels, Moncler is doing an excellent job. The goal is to increase DTC sales, as they involve higher profit margins and greater brand exclusivity. The latter aspect is crucial for high-fashion companies. DTC sales in FY2024 reached 86%, which is 2% higher than last year.

Let us now look at the performance of Stone Island, a very interesting brand but one that has so far disappointed expectations somewhat.

On an annual basis they have disappointed in EMEA and Americas sales, declining 7% and 18% respectively. However, in Q4 2024 they showed a low-mid single-digit recovery and this is promising. In Asia both quarterly and annual growth rates were exceptional, +23% despite a difficult economic environment.

The company is betting heavily on Stone Island and investment will continue to elevate this brand. It may have the potential of Moncler, but you first have to stable a mass culture behind it, and that is not something you can create overnight.

Another allocation of resources and capital is something that we plan to develop on the retail network. But as you know, right now, we want to first to fully to properly develop what we call the retail culture in the existing stores to bring the culture that has developed over the years in Moncler to develop the same culture in Stone Island to make the existing stores more and more productive to work on all the different metrics of retail. And again, it's something that requires a culture that takes time.

Luciano Santel – Chief Corporate and Supply Officer

At the moment, the priority is not to expand Stone Island as much as possible by opening new stores, but to improve the profitability of existing stores. On the other hand, the wholesale distribution channel has declined sharply in favor of the retail channel.

In 2023, 58% of sales were wholesale, by the end of 2024 only 48%. The retail channel is becoming increasingly important and is part of the strategy to elevate the brand.

Overall, the performance of 2024 was quite positive. In fact, revenues increased by 4% while maintaining a very high profit margin of 20.60%. A lot is always expected from such a company and mid-single digit growth may seem disappointing, but you have to put these figures in context with the environment in which Moncler operated in 2024. Only Hermès managed double-digit growth; major brands such as Gucci saw a 23% drop in sales.

Moncler has proven to be a reliable company even in adversity. After all, cash exceeds debt, the competitive advantage is huge especially in China, and free cash flow continues to grow.

In FY 2024 it reached €587 million, up 7% from last year. The cash generated was enough to increase the dividend and further improve the net financial position. But again, this is considered a bad year for haute couture and Moncler can definitely do better in the coming years.

After this sharp rise, Moncler's PE Ratio rose to 28x, not too high for such a company, but I would wait for a retracement to consider it a buy again. Anyway, I have full confidence in its very long-term performance, which is why buying it now is not totally nonsense. My thinking might be influenced by the fact that I bought it a few months ago at a price 20% lower.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Agios Pharmaceuticals: Liver Toxicity Issues May Threaten Thalassemia Approval (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Overview

Agios Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:AGIO), the Cambridge, Massachusetts based biotech, developer of Pyrukund (mitapivat), "an activator of both wild-type and mutant pyruvate kinase, or PK, enzymes for the potential treatment of hemolytic anemias", announced its Q4 and full-year 2024 earnings yesterday.

The company earned $10.7m of revenues in Q4, and made a net loss of $(96.5m), thanks to R&D expenses of $82.8m, and SG&A expenses of $51.7m. For the full-year, revenues were $36.5m, up from $26.8m in the prior year, cost of sales $4.2m, R&D expenses $301m, and SG&A expenses $157m.

Agios reported net income of $674m, however, thanks to a "gain on sale of contingent payments". This relates back to the sale of its oncology division - including isocitrate dehydrogenase-1 (IDH1) inhibitor Tibsovo (vorasidenib), approved in 2017 to treat myeloma, to Servier Pharmaceuticals for $1.8bn back in April 2021.

In August 2024, vorasidenib was approved to treat astrocytoma (a form of brain cancer), and Agios received a $200m milestone payment from Servier as part of the terms of its sale, while the approval also meant Agios was able to complete the sale of its royalty rights in relation to vorasidenib to Royalty Pharma (RPRX), for $905m in cash.

As a result of all this, Agios ended 2024 with a very health cash position of $1.5bn, up from $806.m at the end of 2023.

Pyrukund Agios' New Priority As It Seeks To Expand Label

Agios' focus ever since selling its oncology business has been on Pyrukund, a drug for which it secured approval in the indication of hemolytic anemia in adults with pyruvate kinase ("PK") deficiency back in 2022. Pyrukynd tablets are taken orally twice daily with or without food.

As Agios' revenues imply, Pyrukund's current indication is not a lucrative one, even if revenues in Q4 were up >50% year-on-year, but the company's ambition - which is inching closer to being realised - is to secure approvals for its lead drug in the indications of thalassemia and sickle cell disease ("SCD") - which are multi-billion dollar markets.

Thalassemia is an inherited blood disorder that causes your body to have less hemoglobin than normal, and can cause anemia and extreme fatigue. Agios estimates (in its 2024 annual report / 10K submission) that the disease affects 18k -23k individuals in the US, with ~6k diagnosed patients, ~70k people in Gulf Council countries ("GCC"), and potentially 1m people worldwide.

In sickle cell disease, red blood cells are misshaped, or "sickle" shaped, and can block blood flow as a result, leading to pain crises which often require medical attention or hospitalisation. This is a 120k - 135k patient market in the US, ~150k in GCC, and >3m worldwide.

Pivot, Progress Towards Approvals, & Share Price Turbulence

In June 2021, shortly after the sale of its oncology business was agreed, Agios stock was worth >$60 per share, however by May 2022, it had sunk to a value of <$20, despite Pyrukund's approval in PK deficiency - suggesting the market was less than impressed with Agios' new direction.

Members of senior management left and Agios announced lay-offs and a plan to pivot towards genetic diseases, and appointed a new CEO, Brian Goff, however at the beginning of 2024 shares still traded <$20.

Then, Agios shared positive data from its Phase 3 study of mitapivat in transfusion dependent alpha or beta thalassemia, in which the drug met its primary endpoint, as:

42.3% of patients in the mitapivat arm achieved a hemoglobin response, compared to 1.6% of patients in the placebo arm (2-sided p<0.0001).

Treatment with mitapivat also demonstrated statistically significant improvements compared to placebo for both key secondary endpoints:

  • Change from baseline in average FACIT-Fatigue (Functional Assessment of Chronic Illness Therapy-Fatigue) subscale score from Week 12 to Week 24.
  • Change from baseline in average hemoglobin concentration from Week 12 to Week 24.

After the data were announced, I initiated my coverage of Agios on Seeking Alpha, giving the stock a "Buy" rating - shares are up 48% since that note (at the time of writing).

News of the deal with Royalty Pharma also buoyed Agios' share price, which reached $48 per share in July, and the news that Pfizer's SCD drug Oxbryta, which had earned $328m of revenues in 2023, had been withdraw from the market due to safety issues also seemed to drive upside (despite some analysts concerns that mitapivat could meet a similar fate).

By early December Agios stock had reached a value of >$60 per share, its highest value since 2019, seemingly vindicating the biotech's decision to pursue a new direction, but then the company released its latest Phase 3 thalassemia data, triggering a major market sell-off, and dropping the share price back >$35.

Two patients in the study had experienced liver injury following treatment with mitapivat, the data revealed. Agios disclosed in a press release:

Based on the data from the ENERGIZE and ENERGIZE-T studies, Agios included, in its regulatory applications, hepatocellular injury as an important potential risk of mitapivat in patients with thalassemia and proposed monthly monitoring of liver tests for the first six months of treatment with mitapivat. In addition, mitapivat clinical trial protocols across all indications have been updated to incorporate similar monitoring.

In January, Agios announced that the Food and Drug Agency ("FDA") had accepted its supplemental New Drug Application ("sNDA") for Pyrukund to treat adult patients with non-transfusion-dependent and transfusion-dependent alpha- or beta-thalassemia, and assigned a Prescription Drug User Fee Act ("PDUFA") date - or decision date, in more straightforward terms, of September 7th, 2025.

Meanwhile, Agios stated yesterday in its earning press release that enrollment in its Phase 3 RISE UP study in Sickle Cell Disease had been completed, and that topline data would likely be shared towards the end of 2025.

Analysis - Market Opportunity

We can break down the investment opportunity in relation to Agios today in relatively simple terms. Assuming Agios wins approval for Pyrukund in thalassemia, and sets a list price similar to the price of Oxbryta prior to its withdrawal - ~$125k - and if we use the figure of 6k diagnosed patients - there is a market opportunity of ~$750m in play. If we include undiagnosed patients, that opportunity may rise as high as $2.5bn.

Agios' current market cap is $1.9bn. Besides Pyrukund, approved drugs to treat thalassemia include the gene editing drugs Casgevy, developed by Crispr Therapeutics (CRSP) and Vertex (VRTX), and Zyntelgo, developed by Bluebird Bio (BLUE). I would not consider these therapies rivals of Pyrukund, however, as they are expensive, offer a permanent cure, and treatment can take a year or more, hence, only transfusion dependent patients would likely consider them.

Reblozyl (lustapercept), indicated to treat anemia caused by either myelodysplastic syndromes ("MDS") or thalassemia, might be considered the main market rival - the drug earned >$1.5bn in revenues in 2024 - however it too is indicated for patients wo require regular transfusions.

If we assume a commercial stage biotech typically trades at ~5x sales, assuming it is not heavily loss-making, and that based on the available evidence, Pyrukund has "blockbuster" potential (>$1bn revenues per annum), then we can get to a valuation of ~$5bn for Agios, or >150% higher than today's market cap valuation.

Incidentally, when Pfizer bought out Global Blood Therapeutics, who marketed and sold Oxbryta prior to its withdrawal (what a disastrous deal for Pfizer that was), the deal was valued at $5.4bn.

We should also note that an approval in SCD - pending positive data in 2025 could double or even triple the addressable market / revenue opportunity for Pyrukund, with minimal competition in the market presently.

So far the bull case is taking shape, but what about the safety issues? If pyrukund is rejected for approval, this will likely have a devastating effect on the company's share price. There is a back-up plan in the form of pipeline candidate Tebapivat, a "next-generation" mitapivat, being evaluated in SCD and MDS, but it remains in early stage studies.

We should also note the competition - Agios is not the only company eyeing up the thalassemia / SCD space - according to Agios' 10K submission:

recently approved treatments for thalassemia, SCD, LR MDS, PKU and PV include Reblozyl from Merck (MRK)/BMS (BMY); Revlimid® from BMS; Zynteglo and Lyfgenia from bluebird; Adakveo from Novartis (NVS); Casgevy from Vertex/CRISPR; Kuvan and Palynziq from BioMarin (BMRN); Endari from Emmaus; Besremi from PharmaEssentia; Jakafi from Incyte; and Rytelo from Geron (GERN).

I've discussed reblozyl and the gene editing drugs, but seemingly, Adakveo, Endari, Besremi, and Jakafi could all be pivoted into potential thalassemia or SCD drugs, or be prescribed off-label.

Concluding Thoughts - Based on Available Evidence Is Agios Stock A "Buy", "Sell", or "Hold"?

Personally, I believe it all comes down to the safety question. Clearly, had it not been for the instances of hepatocellular injury observed, the market would still be valuing Agios stock at ~$60 per share, or a market cap of ~$4bn. In its 10K Agios notes:

during the double-blind periods of ENERGIZE and ENERGIZE-T, two patients on PYRUKYND® experienced events of hepatocellular injury. In addition, during the open-label extension periods of both trials, a total of three patients experienced events of hepatocellular injury after switching from placebo to PYRUKYND®. All of these events occurred within the first six months of exposure to PYRUKYND® and liver tests improved following discontinuation of PYRUKYND

Seemingly, then, it was not just two cases of hepatocellular injury, but potentially as many as five. Agios's solution is for patients to be monitored closely during their first six months of Pyrukund, and for treatment to be discontinued if liver injury is observed. Agios says the safety events were:

characterized by a time to onset within the first 6 months of treatment with peak elevations of alanine aminotransferase of >5× upper limit of normal (ULN) with or without jaundice. All patients discontinued treatment with PYRUKYND, and these events improved upon treatment discontinuation.

The liver injuries were potentially life-threatening, even if they improved when use of mitapivat was stopped, and occurred in <2% of all patients receiving the drug in the study period. This is a different issue to that which saw Oxbryta withdrawn - SCD patients were experiencing more pain crises, and more deaths in the treatment group than in the placebo group.

According to a paper I found online shared by the National Library of Medicine:

ALT levels of 5 to 10 times the upper limit of normal (~200 to 400 U/L) without symptoms or jaundice cannot be considered severe hepatotoxicity, and should instead be referred to as “Grade 3 ALT elevations.

As such, I don't see this issue derailing the thalassemia approval push, but it could lead to an unwanted "Black Box" warnings, and make physicians more reluctant to prescribe the drug. Otherwise, mitapivat appeared to a have a decent safety profile - apparently, in the pivotal thalassemia study, "serious treatment-emergent adverse events were reported in 11.0% (n=19) and 15.3% (n=13) of patients on mitapivat and placebo, respectively.

All things considered, then - the fact Pyrukund is already approved, the successful Phase 3, liver injury issues aside, the market opportunity, the paucity of other treatment options, the potential label expansion into Sickle Cell Disease, and the $1.5bn cash position, I'd expect an approval in September, quite possibly followed by a further approval in SCD in 2026.

With that said, the liver toxicity could substantially affect how widespread use of Pyrukund becomes. Reblozyl does not have a Black Box warning, for example, not that I am necessarily suggesting Pyrukund / mitapivat will have one.

In the end, I think it is really tricky to answer the question posed as to whether Agios stock is a "buy", "sell", or "hold, but given safety is the issue, I have to err on the side of caution and suggest a hold. It's possible - although Agios expressed its hope this does not happen - that the FDA will convene an Advisory Committee to discuss the safety issues and vote on whether they should prevent the approval in thalassemia.

In that scenario, we may see a further drop in Agios' share price, so I think its best to monitor progress from the sidelines for the time being, and until there is more clarity.

data.title: JEPI: Losing Its Appeal With This Major Flaw
data.description:
Since writing an article about the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) where I discussed how it was "no longer that attractive," I had received multiple requests for my opinion on the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI), which I will be sharing in this article. I went into my deep analysis of JEPI thoroughly expecting to write a positive article on the fund. Its performance in 2022 and 2023 was undeniably good and showed how the fund's strategy of actively selecting stocks to lower volatility and writing out-of-the-money ("OTM") calls on the S&P 500 via ELNs had led to superior performances during bear markets. In preparation for writing this article, however, deeper analysis suggests that JEPI isn't as good as people make it out to be, especially with the numerous alternative solutions on the market. First, the lower volatility and downside JEPI provides are largely determined by proprietary stock selection strategies that do not necessarily guarantee lower downsides. This is not a good combination with its covered call strategy, which caps JEPI's upsides and makes it more difficult to capture the much-needed recovery gains after large market drops. Distributions from JEPI's ELN options strategy are not particularly stable or desirable, too, as distributions can vary heavily based on volatility and are taxed largely as ordinary income. Although JEPI's performance has been good enough for many income investors and retirees, one should consider opportunity costs. Both GPIX and SPYI are ETFs I prefer over JEPI as they capture upside potential better and distribute more stable payments that reduce the need to lower share price volatility in the first place.

JEPI's Success In 2022

JEPI is an extremely popular fund among retirees due to its strategy of providing large monthly distributions and maintaining lower volatility compared to the overall market. It doesn't matter if JEPI underperforms the overall market in the long run. A key focus for many income investors is to generate income to support their retirement. Share price and capital appreciation are less of a concern so long as the overall portfolio is not going down in value. JEPI is a fund that fits this description, and it showed its worth during the 2022 bear market. JEPI had outperformed the S&P 500 in total returns between January 1, 2022, and December 31, 2023. Indeed, JEPI's volatility has also been consistently lower than SPY since its inception.

There are two fundamental components to JEPI's strategy that have helped the fund achieve these results. The first component is JEPI's active selection of stocks that lower volatility. What you'll notice when you first look at JEPI's holdings is that their sector concentrations are very different from the S&P 500. For example, technology only occupies 19.33% of JEPI's portfolio versus the 32.70% in SPY. This smaller concentration of technology in JEPI has been spread across the other sectors, leading to less sector risk.

Moreover, JEPI does not select all of the S&P 500's constituents and holds its stocks in more equal-weight positions. Its top holding currently, META, only occupies 1.80% of the portfolio versus the same stock taking a 3.08% position in SPY. Not to mention that SPY's top holding, AAPL, occupies nearly 7% of the portfolio. By taking more of an equal-weight approach, JEPI has managed to lower single-stock risk significantly.

The second component of JEPI's strategy that has helped lower its volatility significantly is its strategy of selling covered calls on the S&P 500 via ELNs. This is also the component that generates most of JEPI's monthly distribution. The reason selling covered calls can help lower volatility is that the option premiums generated by covered calls increase with volatility.

With these two components of active selection of lower volatility stocks and covered calls, you can see how JEPI navigated the 2022 bear market so well compared to the overall market. A more evenly-distributed portfolio among sectors in JEPI helped neutralize some of the big losses driven by tech and consumer discretionary stocks in the market cap-weighted S&P 500. Additionally, increased volatility led to increased option premiums from JEPI's ELNs, which offset losses significantly, with 2022 being JEPI's year with the biggest payouts.

In theory, JEPI is a fund that will drop less than the overall market while simultaneously increasing its payouts during downturns, and based on what we saw in 2022, this theory has shown to work so far. Why then, am I rating JEPI negatively?

The Importance of Capturing Upside In Covered Call Funds

The problem with JEPI is that downside protection is not guaranteed. When we look at JEPIX, the mutual fund that implements the same strategy as JEPI, we can see how the fund would have performed during the 2020 pandemic crash. What you'll see is that JEPIX crashed almost as much as SPY.

This becomes a significant concern when considering that the biggest risk with selling covered calls is the capped upside, especially on the recovery. Keep in mind that if your asset loses 20% in value, it'll take a 25% gain to recover to its original price. If JEPI can drop just as much as the S&P 500 but doesn't have the same growth prospects, it will naturally lead to long-term underperformance.

Inefficient, Variable Distributions

Of course, what's more important about JEPI is its monthly distributions. This is supposed to be a fund that retirees can buy without thinking much and live off the dividend payments. There are a few problems with this approach, though. While yields have typically ranged around 7% and as much as 12%, distribution amounts change frequently with volatility and JEPI's underlying holdings.

For reference, JEPI's distributions have a standard deviation level of 9.31%, while competitor funds like GPIX and SPYI have distribution standard deviation levels of 1.64% and 2.12%, respectively. In other words, you can frequently expect JEPI's distribution to change close to 10% every month on average. This brings up a big discussion point for retirees and income investors. Would you prefer to reduce volatility in your underlying portfolio or your income?

For most income investors, reducing volatility in income makes more sense than reducing volatility in the overall portfolio. So long as the portfolio values recover, having a stable income makes day-to-day financial planning far easier.

JEPI's distributions are also primarily ordinary income for those investing in taxable accounts, with small portions classified as qualified dividend income due to their ELNs. Essentially, those investing in non tax-sheltered accounts will find their variable distributions even more difficult to manage. Ordinary income taxes are typically the highest tax rates and with yields that can get as low as 6%, you are getting into territories where stocks paying qualified dividends can lead to more money in your pockets after taxes. If you make $60K a year, for example, 6.6% yielding VZ would lead to more money than a 7.16% yielding JEPI due to the 15% tax rate on qualified dividends versus the 22% ordinary income tax rate. Keep in mind that the tax rate on qualified dividends maxes out at 20% when you start making more than $500K, while ordinary income tax rates scale up to as much as 37% as your income rises. That said, please note that this is not tax advice, and it is always recommended to consult a professional tax advisor for your specific situation. Tax laws and regulations vary from state to state, not to mention from country to country.

Buy GPIX and SPYI Instead of JEPI

While there once was a time when JEPI was the king of covered call funds, that is no longer the case today with the numerous funds that have launched since. If we only consider funds that sell monthly covered calls, GPIX and SPYI are much better alternatives than JEPI for most income investors.

First, their distributions are much more stable. GPIX targets an 8.5% distribution rate, while SPYI targets a 12% distribution rate. Comparatively, JEPI's yields range as high as 12.23% or as low as 6.59%. This, however, isn't considering that both SPYI and GPIX have the capability of offering more tax-efficient distributions through using tax-loss harvesting strategies to produce return of capital ("ROC") distributions that lower your cost-basis in the ETF and defer taxes until you sell or when your cost-basis reaches zero. SPYI also has the benefit of using 1256 index contracts that qualify for 60/40 long-term short-term capital gains tax rates. Thus, you are more likely to end up with more income in your pockets investing in GPIX or SPYI instead of JEPI, especially if you are factoring in taxes.

Of course, the risk is that GPIX and SPYI have not been around nearly as long as JEPI and are smaller than JEPI. GPIX was launched in October 2023 and has $482M in AUM while SPYI was launched in August 2022 and has over $3.02B in AUM. Both pale in comparison to JEPI, which was launched in May 2020 and has amassed over $39B in AUM. This limited history means we don't get to see how GPIX or SPYI performed during the 2022 bear market nor the 2020 pandemic crash (which we got to see how JEPI may have performed via JEPIX). Fortunately, GPIX and SPYI's strategies are much more transparent. Their call options are managed themselves, and the underlying holdings simply track the S&P 500. During down markets, we can expect the two funds to fall almost just as much as the S&P 500, with the only thing offsetting the losses being call option premiums.

The benefit, however, is that GPIX and SPYI are far better at capturing upside than JEPI. So even if the funds drop more than JEPI, we can expect the two funds to capture the recovery upside much better than JEPI, especially considering that GPIX and SPYI will have an easier time reaching their target distributions when volatility is high and can open up larger portions of their portfolio for capital appreciation. Keep in mind that JEPI is not guaranteed to reduce volatility significantly too. The pandemic crash showed how JEPI could drop as much as the S&P 500, and in cases where losses are not preventable, you'd rather position yourself for the recovery.

Essentially, GPIX and SPYI offer more upside than JEPI and more stable income that's often higher and more tax-efficient. When these two funds exist, not to mention other strong-performing S&P 500 covered call funds like various 0DTE funds, it has become difficult for me to justify buying JEPI. Yes, JEPI offers lower volatility and has proven itself to do so during 2022. This lower volatility is not guaranteed, however, as the fund can still drop just as much as the overall market as shown by how the mutual fund version, JEPIX, performed during the 2020 pandemic crash. Combine this with the volatility and tax inefficiency of JEPI's distributions, and it becomes difficult to continue justify holding JEPI. Thus, I rate JEPI a Sell and recommend income investors look at GPIX and SPYI as alternatives. I have written multiple pieces on the two funds linked below as initial points of research.

GPIX Vs. SPYI: How To Choose The Right Fund For You

GPIX: Capturing More Upside Than Other Monthly S&P 500 Buy-Write Funds

SPYI: Buy This For The 12% Target Distributions

data.title: IBM Is Missing The AI Hype
data.description:
Recently, I added Microsoft (MSFT) to my coverage and observed that we saw strong growth in cloud and AI. That made me wonder how companies such as IBM are doing with their cloud and AI solutions, and what I can already say is that growth we see at IBM is nowhere close to what Microsoft is achieving. Both companies have different scale, but IBM doesn’t come even close to the growth rates we're seeing at Microsoft. In this report, I will be discussing the most recent earnings and why IBM is showing weaker growth.

IBM Results Do Not Reflect AI Growth

Full-year revenues grew 1.4% to $62.75 billion, primarily driven by the Software and Consulting segments, partially offset by lower infrastructure, financing and other revenues. Gross profit increased 3.6% to $35.6 billion, driven by higher margins for software and consulting and the revenue mix also leaning more toward those segments. Due to a non-cash pension settlement charge in the third quarter, the other income flipped from a $914 million income to a $1.87 billion expense. Combined with higher R&D and S,G&A this let to EBIT dropping from $8.7 billion to $5.8 billion. On an adjusted basis, earnings grew from $10.3 billion to $11.2 billion or 8.7%, which I believe is good performance given the marginally higher growth in sales.

Software revenues grew 9% driven by 12% growth in Red Hat which provides open hybrid cloud solutions, and 15% growth in Automation. Data & AI revenues grew at a modest rate of just 2%, and that shows AI is not a growth driver for all companies. Furthermore, security sales were up 1% and transaction processing sales were up 10%. Segment profits grew 16% to $8.7 billion.

In the Consulting segment, revenues were up 1% driven by business transformation sales partially offset by technology consulting and application with segment profit declining 4% to $2.1 billion. The infrastructure segment saw revenues drop 3% to $14 billion with a 2% decline in Hybrid Infrastructure sales to $8.9 billion driven by IBM Z, which is the company’s family of mainframe computers. Infrastructure Support sales dropped 3% to $5.1 billion. Segment profits dropped 13% to $2.4 billion.

Why Is IBM Barely Benefiting From Growing AI And Cloud Demand?

After looking at the results, I wondered why IBM is not seeing huge growth in Cloud & AI, and the reason seems to be that while other companies focus on public cloud solutions and a mix of enterprise and consumer clients, IBM is focusing on tailored solution, and its hybrid solution, which combines on-premises infrastructure and public cloud services, is not as scalable as public cloud solutions. With more enterprises moving to cloud solutions, the infrastructure segment is seeing its sales decline and IBM is not the go-to cloud provider as it focuses more on customized solutions. So, whereas Microsoft focuses on public cloud services to consumers and enterprises, IBM has been more focused on enterprises and custom enterprise solutions as well as hybrid cloud services for which demand is simply slower. I believe that's the reason why we do not see IBM making big jumps in revenues on AI and cloud demand. It simply does not have the product offering that clients are looking for, and as a result, enterprises are looking more for cloud solutions from Amazon (AWS), Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG) (GGOLG) and Oracle (ORCL).

No Compelling Investment Angle For IBM

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

IBM is not the stock that's a frontrunner in the AI growth cycle. The stock, however, seemingly has been priced as if IBM is seeing big growth. That's not the case. The company outlined a strategy for 5% annual growth in free cash flow consistent with our modeling for EBITDA and free cash flow. That, however, does not provide upside in any of the future years. So, IBM is more a stock to hold for some stable dividend income as I'm doing and hope that its acquisition path bolster the revenues. There's upside in the growth rate of the Software segment, but it does not do much to meaningfully accelerate growth. As a result, I do believe that the stock is a hold.

Conclusion: IBM Does Not Have What It Takes Yet

I believe that IBM is definitely not one of the companies that one should invest in for AI growth prospects. These days we see companies plugging AI wherever they can, but the reality is that some companies barely have any meaningful upside driven by AI, and in my view IBM is one of those companies. The only way in which I see better growth opportunities is by accelerating acquisitions.

data.title: Crexendo's Bullish Run: Capitalizing On Microsoft's Exit And Cloud Demand
data.description:
Crexendo (NASDAQ:CXDO) was one of the first few stocks I covered on the Seeking Alpha platform, and in retrospect, it turned out to be one of my most successful calls. Since my last coverage, the stock’s up more than 27% and is still running hot. In that particular piece, I talked extensively about Crexendo’s position as a top-tier Unified Communications as a Service (UCaaS) player that benefits from an integrated approach. Moreover, I discussed Crexendo’s steady expansion into high-margin software solutions and its dependable telecom services that collectively bolster its top-and-bottom-line. Over the past five months, these elements have shined for the company, along with the Microsoft exit adding new layers to its growth story. Nonetheless, it's important to acknowledge the stellar run-up in value, and the potential for those tailwinds to be fully priced in. However, I feel that there’s still room to run for the stock given the technical indicators and the spillover effects of Microsoft’s (MSFT) exit from its niche. Hence, for now, I am maintaining my Buy rating on Crexendo stock, leaving it unchanged from last time.

Sustained Momentum in Q3

In my previous article which mostly covered Crexendo’s Q2 results, I highlighted the company’s exceptional performances across both lines. At that point, it had posted its fifth consecutive quarter of beats, backed by healthy YOY gains. Five months later, it's mostly glowing reports, with another comprehensive beat across both lines in Q3. Mind you, Q3 2023 was a high point for the company, with $13.9 million in sales (a 52.3% YOY increase) and EPS of $0.12, beating estimates by 10 cents. Nonetheless, despite the tough comps, Crexendo mostly sustained its momentum, while highlighting a few shifts on a QOQ basis.

Q3 sales came in at $15.6 million representing a 13% bump YOY, which brings its nine-month tally to $44.6 million (up 14% from last year’s $39 million). As expected, the star of the show was its Software Solutions (SS) segment, posting a 25% YOY increase, and 28% growth over a nine-month period. Similarly, we saw Service revenues rise 6%, strengthening the narrative of higher-margin, subscription-based offerings driving its top-line expansion.

On the profitability front though, we saw net income for the quarter dropping to $0.1 million in Q3, from $1.7 million in the prior-year period. However, over a nine-month period, its net income turned positive at $1.2 million, reversing a $0.4 million loss, highlighting the strength of its overall performance.

In terms of liquidity, we saw a healthy increase in Crexendo’s cash balance, which jumped to $15.5 million during the quarter, up remarkably from $10.3 million at the year-end. Also, the company’s backlog continues moving in the right direction, although at a relatively measured pace compared to Q2. As of Q3, the combined backlog stood at $77.4 million, reflecting a 22% YOY bump, which despite being lower compared to Q2, still underscores a robust pipeline. Additionally, the company projects that a sizeable portion of these booked sales will be realized within the next couple of years.

As we look ahead, the analyst consensus projects Crexendo’s sales to hit roughly $60.2 million in fiscal 2024, a 13% YOY surge, followed by $66.7 million in 2025 and $74.5 million in 2026. The numbers point to a consistent double-digit top-line growth between 10% and 12% annually, backed by stellar demand for its UCaaS solutions. Also, based on its forward price-to-sales ratio, the stock is set to trade more attractively, declining from 2.68 in 2024 to 2.16 by 2026.

Industry Outlook: AI Growth and Microsoft’s Departure

In an environment, where you have businesses continuing to invest in cost-effective, secure, and scalable communication solutions, Crexendo remains in an excellent position to be carving out an even larger slice of the booming UCaaS market. The UCaaS market is riding multiple secular tailwinds, with the shift to distributed and hybrid work remaining as sticky as ever. Despite some high-profile calls to return to the office, the need for flexible, integrated communications is firmly intact. Unsurprisingly, estimates such as from Mordor Intelligence, expect the market to jump from $44.68 billion this year, to a whopping $139.94 billion by 2029, with a 26% CAGR.

A key catalyst for the industry will be AI and machine learning, which will play a critical role in improving routing efficiency, streamlining customer support, and offering real-time language translation, along with more insights than ever before.

From Crexendo’s Q3 investor presentation, we see that it’s already integrating AI into its platform, to unlock long-term growth potential while lowering costs. These AI-powered enhancements focus on improving customer and employee experiences, from AI-driven messaging and research tools to AI assistants for application building.

Furthermore, the company’s spending patterns over the past few years underscore the need for AI-driven cost reductions. Though its gross margin as a percentage of sales is healthy at 60% TTM, its total operational expenses have historically formed a sizeable percentage of sales. We can see from the snapshot that despite its bottom-line improvements, a large chunk of sales still accrues to operational costs (well over 50% of total revenues). Moreover, the transition to Oracle (ORCL) Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) and the migration of Crexendo Classic customers to its VIP system will also have a positive impact on its bottom-line.

Perhaps Crexendo's biggest catalyst is the potential windfall from Microsoft ending support for its Metaswitch MaX UC platform. Consequently, you have a situation where hundreds of Microsoft’s enterprise and service providers need to find alternative platforms. Given Crexendo’s proven cloud infrastructure and high customer satisfaction, it’s arguably the most viable Microsoft substitute.

Crexendo CEO Jeff Korn highlighted the company’s “strategic efforts to attract Cisco and Microsoft customers” during the Q3 earnings call, mentioning a webinar that drew 180 attendees. Notably, Crexendo has already secured four MetaSwitch contracts in the past four months. Korn emphasized that this isn’t a one-time transition but an ongoing opportunity, with clients gradually migrating to Crexendo’s platform. Therefore, we’re looking at a massive new opportunity for Crexendo, tightening the noose on the UCaaS market.

Upside Potential Remains in Play

Crexendo stock has been on an impressive run over the past few months, surging from mid-/late September last year near the $4.40–$4.50 range to about $6.00–$6.10 by mid-February. It is currently hovering above the 20-week EMA (in orange) at $5.40 and the 50-week EMA (in yellow) at $4.81. Additionally, we’ve had multiple weekly closes above the 20-week EMA, which reinforces the uptrend. Also, after running up swiftly from $4.40 to the upper band near $5.50–$6.00, the Bollinger Bands have widened slightly but the price continues holding near the middle or upper band. On top of that the OBV line (in purple), has held up well, which implies consistent buying pressure, with the Relative Strength Index (RSI) suggesting mildly overbought conditions. All in all, the indicators suggest that there’s still room for the stock to climb, with the fundamentals continuing to support its growth story.

Moreover, in evaluating Crexendo’s valuation, I recalculated its fair value based on updates to its weighted average cost of capital (WACC). The key change is the higher long-term risk-free rate, reflecting recent developments, which in turn lowered the equity risk premium. Additionally, I used a 5-year monthly beta of 1.15, and with the increase in market cap, the after-tax WACC came in at 9.97%. Based on the new WACC, the results of the sensitivity analysis are shown below.

As shown, with Crexendo’s levered FCF margin of 13.2% and its current WACC, the stock still has ample upside potential. However, sustaining a double-digit FCF growth rate in the 15%-20% range will be key for even greater gains. Additionally, optimizing its financing structure to keep WACC below 10% remains critical for long-term upside.

Risks To The Thesis

Despite the compelling bull case for Crexendo, there are still multiple threats that could derail its growth story. Firstly, it has a decent reliance on acquisitions in driving expansion, which carries a fair share of integration risks. If the acquired firms struggle to mesh from an operational standpoint or fail in delivering synergistic gains, overall profitability will take a hit. Secondly, while Microsoft’s exit from the UCaaS market creates tailwinds, there are plenty of deep-pocketed competitors with aggressive pricing strategies that continue to pressure its margins and market share gains. Also, if its AI enhancements fall short of expectations, the company could struggle to meet its objectives and fall significantly behind the competition.

Takeaway On Crexendo Stock

Crexendo continues to build on its robust fundamentals, backed by sustained revenue growth and a healthy backlog. The new opportunities from Microsoft’s recent retreat and the organic demand for hybrid and remote solutions highlight a compelling market position. While the competitive pressures and execution risks exist, Crexendo’s integrated approach and focus on high-margin offerings give it a sizeable edge over its competition. Moreover, despite the recent run-up in value, it still offers decent upside potential based on the technicals and my DCF model centered around realistic assumptions. Hence, for those who can tolerate short-term volatility, Crexendo remains an enticing long-term play.

data.title: Robot Consulting Pursues US IPO On High Valuation
data.description:
Robot Consulting Is Growing But Seeking A High Valuation

Robot Consulting Co., Ltd. (LAWR) has filed to raise $12.5 million in an IPO of its ordinary shares, according to an SEC F-1 registration statement.

Robot Consulting provides human resource software to companies seeking to track employee attendance and sales orders.

Given the firm’s small size, high valuation expectations, high operating losses, plans to expand into other markets and considerable risks, my outlook on the Robot Consulting IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Robot Consulting Do?

Tokyo, Japan-based Robot Consulting Co., Ltd. was founded to develop a software system to aid small and medium businesses in tracking their employee's activities.

The firm seeks to expand into adjacent areas of legal technology and the metaverse in the future.

Management is headed by founder and Chairman Mr. Hidetoshi Yokoyama, who has been with the firm since its inception in April 2020 and was previously in sales at temporary staffing company TRY NET and as an advisor for a variety of certified consultant corporations.

The company’s primary offering is its Labor Robot, a cloud-based employee attendance and sales order tracking software system.

As of September 30, 2024, Robot Consulting has booked fair market value investment of $7.4 million from investors, including Stella MIC Investment and the founder.

The firm sells its system primarily through distributors, the largest of which is Nac, a listed company in Japan that has acted as the company's primary distributor.

Robot Consulting receives 7% of the total revenue that Nac obtains from the sale of its system.

In the six months ended September 30, 2024, the firm obtained only 4.2% of its revenue from Nac's customers.

Selling, G&A costs as a function of topline revenue have dropped as revenues have grown, as the figures below indicate:

Market Overview For Robot Consulting

According to a 2025 market research report by Grand View Research, the Japanese enterprise software market was an estimated $12.6 billion in 2024 and is expected to reach nearly $26 billion by 2030.

If accomplished, this growth would represent a forecast CAGR of 12.8% from 2025 to 2030.

The primary reason for this expected growth is the continued demand for enterprise resource planning [ERP] software as the most sought-after enterprise software segment within the industry

IPO Details For Robot Consulting

Robot Consulting intends to raise $12.5 million in gross proceeds from an IPO of its ordinary shares, offering 2.5 million shares at a proposed midpoint price of $5.00.

No existing shareholders have shown a desire to purchase additional shares in the IPO.

With a completed IPO, the firm’s enterprise value would be approximately $215 million, not including the effects of underwriter over-allotment options.

The float to outstanding shares ratio (ex-underwriter over-allotments) will be approximately 5.6%, which means the stock will be a ‘low float’ stock and subject to potentially higher volatility in open market trading.

Management said the dilution in net tangible book value per share for investors in the IPO is expected to be $3.92, which is within the typical range for a company at IPO.

The company and shareholders will be subject to a standard 180-day shareholder sale or issuance lock-up period from the closing of the offering.

Management disclosed it intends to apply the net proceeds from the IPO as follows:

approximately 35% for recruiting talent and increasing the number of employees;

approximately 30% for investing in the research and development of our new and existing products;

approximately 10% for investing in equipment and facilities, including expanding our office to accommodate additional employees; and

approximately 25% for working capital and general corporate proposes.

(Source - SEC)

The company's online roadshow is not available.

Regarding outstanding legal proceedings, the company has an outstanding judgment against it of approximately $144,000 that it is considering appealing.

The listed bookrunners of the IPO are D. Boral Capital and Craft Capital Management.

Robot Consulting Is A Small Company Seeking A Big Valuation

LAWR is seeking US public capital market investment to fund its expansion plans into other markets.

The firm’s financials have produced increasing topline revenue, higher gross profit and gross margin, and lower operating losses but growing cash used in operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended September 30, 2024, was negative ($1.8 million).

Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue have dropped as revenue has grown; its Selling, G&A efficiency multiple fell to 0.3x in the most recent reporting period, a relatively low figure.

The firm currently intends to pay zero dividends and will likely keep any future earnings and cash flow for its growth and expansion initiatives.

Robot Consulting’s recent capital spending results show it has continued to spend on capital expenditures even as its operating cash flow has remained negative.

The market opportunity for providing software to businesses in Japan is large and expected to grow at a relatively robust rate of growth in the coming years, so the company enjoys positive industry growth dynamics in its favor.

The company will have risks as a public entity, including its ‘foreign private issuer’ and ‘emerging growth company’ status, which allows management to disclose substantially less information to shareholders.

Many of these types of company stocks have fared poorly after their IPO.

Also, when a company indicates it will use the IPO proceeds to move into other markets, that suggests management believes the existing market has been largely tapped out and that the existing focus is insufficient for their future efforts.

Management is seeking an EV/Revenue multiple of approximately 37.1x on a high revenue growth rate but from a tiny revenue base.

Given the firm’s small size, high valuation expectations, heavy operating losses, plans to expand into other markets and considerable risks, my outlook on the Robot Consulting IPO is to sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Arthur J. Gallagher: Interesting Growth Prospects Are Reflected In The Share Price
data.description:
Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. (NYSE:AJG) is a growing company in the insurance brokerage industry, but this seems to be already reflected in its share price. As this is my first coverage on Gallagher, in this article I do an overview of its business profile, then review its most recent financial performance. I will then analyze its financial leverage and valuation to see if it offers value for long-term investors.

Business Overview

Gallagher is part of the insurance sector, but contrary to other companies that I’ve covered in the past, it has a somewhat different business profile. The company provides insurance brokerage, reinsurance brokerage, consulting, and third-party property & casualty (P&C) claims settlement and administration services. This means its business is more focused on consulting rather than taking insurance risk, being therefore a capital-light business model as the company does not assume underwriting risk. Its major competitors are other companies operating in the brokerage insurance industry, such as AON Plc (AON) or Willis Towers Watson (WTW).

Gallagher has a long experience in the industry given that the company was founded in 1927, being nowadays one of the world’s largest insurance brokers. Its shares are traded on the New York Stock Exchange and its current market value is about $82 billion, having a similar size by this measure compared to Aon and is much larger than Willis Towers Watson.

It operates in some 130 countries and has close to 55,000 employees, even though the vast majority of its revenues are still generated domestically. Its business is based on two key segments, namely the brokerage and the risk management segments, with some 87% of revenue being generated in brokerage and 13% in risk management.

In the brokerage segment, it provides brokerage and consulting services to different types of clients, including commercial, public-sector entities, insurance companies, beyond others. It also provides employer sponsored benefit programs, being an area that is more related to human resources management. The vast majority of its revenue is commission or fee-based, providing a recurring revenue stream over the long term. In the risk segment, it provides claim settlement operations, claim administration, loss control services, and risk management consulting for different types of businesses that choose to use a third-party claims management provider.

In both segments, the company does not take much underwriting risk, except for minimum amounts necessary to provide regulatory capital to organize captives, pools, specialized underwritings or risk-retention groups.

As shown in the next graph, its business diversification is acceptable even though it’s somewhat concentrated in retail P&C in the brokerage segment, and workers’ compensation in risk management. Geographically, both segments are quite exposed to the domestic market, a profile that is not expected to change much in the foreseeable future.

Regarding its growth strategy, the company has grown historically both organically and through acquisitions, a strategy that is expected to be maintained in the future. Recently, its growth has been quite strong, as the company has delivered good top-line growth and improved business margins, supporting a strong share price rally over the past five years (+220% compared to 95% for the S&P 500 index).

More recently, the company has maintained its strategy of growing through acquisitions, namely by its agreement reached last December to buy the insurance broker AssuredPartners in an all-cash deal valued at $13.45 billion. That is expected to close in the short term. This will strengthen Gallagher’s operations in retail middle-market P&C and employee benefits in the United States. Internationally, it is also increasing its presence in the U.K. and Ireland, being expected to have integration costs of about $500 million and generate synergies of around $160 million.

AssuredPartners generates annual revenues of about $2.9 billion and EBITDA above $900 million, representing an EBITDA margin of around 32%, which is quite close to Gallagher’s business margin. Gallagher will pay a multiple of about 14.3x EBITDA, which is a lower valuation multiple than compared to Gallagher’s own valuation (closer to 17x EBITDA), and the deal is expected to be accretive to its EPS by 10-12% on a trailing twelve-month basis.

Gallagher’s assumptions regarding revenue and cost synergies in this deal appear to be somewhat conservative, and the multiple paid seem quite attractive. This means this seems to be a good deal for Gallagher to improve its position in the industry and enhance its operating efficiency in the coming years. Going forward, Gallagher should maintain its growth strategy of performing bolt-on acquisitions on top of its organic growth efforts, which should allow it to maintain strong top-line and earnings growth over the coming years.

Financial Overview

Regarding its financial performance, Gallagher has a very good track record, as the company has been able to grow consistently its revenues and earnings recently. This has been supported by acquisitions, but also by favorable conditions in the insurance over the past couple of years. Pricing has been strong in the U.S. commercial market, allowing the company to report high-single-digit organic growth rates lately.

As shown in the previous graph, while its organic growth is clearly related to overall pricing in the insurance industry, Gallagher has consistently outperformed the market throughout several cycles. This shows that its business model is less cyclical than compared to traditional insurance carriers.

More recently, its organic growth has been closer to overall pricing trends in the U.S. commercial markets, being an important support for revenue growth in recent quarters. In 2024, its total revenues amounted to $11.34 billion, representing an increase of 14.4% compared to the previous year. By segment, brokerage reported revenues of nearly $9.9 billion (+14.5% YOY), while its risk segment revenues were $1.45 billion (+12.8% YOY), boosted both by organic growth and acquisitions.

Its EBITDA increased by 19% YOY to more than $3.5 billion, leading to an improvement of more than 100 basis points in its EBITDA margin during the year, to 31.5% in 2024.

This good operating performance is not expected to change much in the near term. The global P&C insurance pricing environment remains strong, even though there is some deceleration in specific lines, such as property and professional lines, which have reported flat pricing recently. Overall, pricing remains up across most lines and geographies, boding well for Gallagher’s organic growth in 2025.

Taking this background into consideration, its guidance is to achieve organic revenue growth in the 6-8% range in the brokerage segment in 2025, maintaining its positive trend in recent years. In the risk segment, it’s also positive about the business outlook. The U.S. labor market remains strong and clients are increasingly investigating how to retain talent and control cost growth, expecting to reach 6-8% annual organic growth as well, and a stable business margin compared to 2024.

Regarding M&A, beyond the AssuredPartners acquisition, Gallagher continues to have a pipeline of small acquisitions, and had at the time of its 2024 earnings release some 45 tuck-in deals in the pipeline, which could increase its annual revenues by some $650 million. This means its total revenues could reach about $12.9 billion in 2025 without the integration of AssuredPartners, representing annual growth of around 13%. Assuming the acquisition of AssuredPartners is approved and completed by the end of Q1 2025, its total revenues this year can reach close to $15 billion, up by 32% compared to 2024.

In addition to higher revenues, the company also sees margin expansion ahead, which can be between 50 and 100 basis points, depending on how much organic revenue grows. The company is expected to perform an investor day next June, in which it is likely to disclose more details about synergies in the AssuredPartners deal and how other deals and organic growth are impacting its margin expansion this year.

Regarding its financial position, Gallagher had more than $14 billion of cash at the end of 2024, after raising some $8.5 billion through a capital increase and $5 billion in bonds to fund the AssuredPartners acquisition, which will represent a cash outflow of $13.5 billion. However, after year-end it received more than $1.3 billion as the underwriters of its equity offering exercised the green shoe and due to its strong operating cash flow generation, it expects to have about $3.5 billion to fund M&A during 2025.

Despite having ambitions to continue to perform M&A transactions in the future, the company also wants to maintain an investment-grade credit rating in the medium to long term. This means its leverage ratio, measured by the net debt-to-EBITDA ratio, should be between 2.5x-3x over the medium term. At the end of 2024, this ratio was negative as the company raised equity and debt to finance its AssuredPartners acquisition, but this profile is expected to change when the deal closes.

Given that its total debt at the end 2024 was around $13.2 billion, and its EBITDA should be around $4.7 billion in 2025 with the consolidation of AssuredPartners, its leverage ratio should be 2.8x at the end of 2025. This is under the assumption that Gallagher uses all annual cash flow generation to finance M&A and distribute dividends.

Investors should note that Gallagher has clearly a preference to reinvest cash flow generation in acquisitions rather than returning much capital to shareholders, and this strategy is not expected to change much in the future. Its dividend is rather small at only at €2.60 per share, which at its current share price leads to a dividend yield of only 0.8%. Thus, Gallagher’s income appeal is quite low, and it’s not a reason to buy its shares in my opinion, a profile that is likely to remain the same in the foreseeable future.

Regarding its valuation, Gallagher is currently trading at 27.5x forward earnings and 16.7x EV/EBITDA, which are both metrics above its historical valuation over the past five years of 24x and 15.3x times, respectively. Compared to its peers, Gallagher is also trading at a premium valuation. This means that its shares appear to be currently fairly valued, even though upside over the medium to long term is likely to come from business growth, as Gallagher is likely to make further acquisitions over the coming years.

Conclusion

Arthur J. Gallagher is a company with an interesting position in the insurance brokerage market, having good growth prospects both organically and through acquisitions. Its expected acquisition of AssuredPartners is another step to make Gallagher a leading player in the industry, boding well for its business prospects over the long term. Despite this background, its shares aren’t cheap currently compared to its historical multiples and peers, thus my recommendation is “hold”
for the time being.

data.title: RSPT: Technology Dashboard For February
data.description:
This monthly article series reports industry metrics in information technology. It may also serve as a top-down analysis of sector ETFs like Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK) and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight Technology ETF (NYSEARCA:RSPT), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield (“EY”), Sales Yield (“SY”), Free Cash Flow Yield (“FY”), Return on Equity (“ROE”), Gross Margin (“GM”). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the “something” is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for hardware in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in hardware companies.

The Value Score (“VS”) is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios ((EY, SY, FY)) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score (“QS”) is the average difference between the two quality ratios ((ROE, GM)) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.
